tools.name: Cursor
tools.pricing: Free tier; Pro $20/mo; Teams seat pricing
tools.one_liner: The most polished agentic IDE and the daily-driver favorite.
tools.blurb: A VS Code fork built around AI. Composer 2.5 and parallel agents make multi-file edits and refactors fast and fluid, and it runs multiple models so you can match the model to the task.
cons: Usage costs can climb on heavy agent use

tools.name: Claude Code
tools.one_liner: Highest capability ceiling for complex, autonomous work.
tools.blurb: A terminal-native agent that works alongside any editor. Powered by Opus 4.8, it leads SWE-Bench Verified and is strongest on deep, multi-file reasoning and large-codebase tasks.
pros: Strongest reasoning on complex, multi-file work
cons: Terminal-first, steeper ramp than an IDE
Can get pricey at heavy usage

tools.name: GitHub Copilot
tools.blurb: The most adopted tool, with the deepest IDE coverage (VS Code, JetBrains, Neovim), IP indemnity, and strong admin controls. Lower capability ceiling than dedicated agents.
cons: New usage-based flex billing drew backlash

tools.name: OpenAI Codex
tools.one_liner: A powerful standalone cloud coding agent.
tools.blurb: A cloud agent on GPT-5.5 with macOS and Windows desktop apps, plus Sites, Annotations, and business plugins. Strong autonomous runs; newer as a standalone product.

tools.name: Windsurf (Devin Desktop)
tools.one_liner: Agentic IDE with a built-in cloud agent.
tools.blurb: An agentic IDE that bundles the Devin cloud agent for one-click delegation to a remote VM. Recently rebranded to Devin Desktop, so expect some transition churn.

tools.name: Google Antigravity 2.0
tools.one_liner: Agent-first, multi-agent suite — the newest contender.
tools.blurb: An agent-first IDE on Gemini 3.5 Flash with dynamic subagents, scheduled background tasks, a Go-based CLI, and a public SDK. The youngest of the serious tools, still proving itself.
cons: Value depends on a Google AI subscription
